summary:
Submental intubation is indicated in patients with midface or panfacial fractures where anatomical disruption prevents standard intubation methods. This makes it a preferred alternative over tracheostomy in severe facial trauma affecting the upper airway structure rather than isolated mandibular injuries.  

Note: Only include information strictly related to the clinical relevance of the intervention based on available medical sources; avoid generalizations not supported by retrieval results.

2) Content focus: Extract only those parts discussing direct links between submental intubation and types of facial fractures using explicit references found in their document review—for example mentioning "Le Fort," “midface,” or “panfacial” fractures—as these represent actual findings derived from search outcomes.

3) Eliminate irrelevant details: Remove any discussion unrelated to fracture classification (e.g., procedural steps unless they tie into diagnosis/indication). Avoid repeating conclusions already stated elsewhere without adding new insight.

4) Maintain strict neutrality & accuracy: Do NOT introduce opinions outside documented support (“strongly suggested”, etc.) nor generalize beyond retrievals.

5) Final output must be exactly two complete sentences, each containing one clear point grounded entirely in retrieved content regarding clinical applicability per fracture category.

→ Evidence states it applies during midface/pandecial trauma when anatomy disrupts normal pathways
